***General Hospital*** (often abbreviated as ***GH***) is an American daytime television [soap opera](/wiki/Soap_opera "Soap opera") created by [Frank and Doris Hursley](/wiki/Frank_and_Doris_Hursley "Frank and Doris Hursley") which has been broadcast on [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") since April 1, 1963. Originally a half-hour serial, its running time was expanded from 30 minutes to 45 minutes on July 26, 1976, and again to a full hour on January 16, 1978.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Set in a [hospital](/wiki/Hospital "Hospital") in the city of Port Charles, New York,[[a]](#cite_note-3) *General Hospital* originally starred [John Beradino](/wiki/John_Beradino "John Beradino") and [Emily McLaughlin](/wiki/Emily_McLaughlin "Emily McLaughlin"); both actors stayed with the show until their deaths in 1996 and 1991, respectively. They were joined a year later by [Rachel Ames](/wiki/Rachel_Ames "Rachel Ames") who made her most recent appearance in 2015. The show is taped at [the Prospect Studios](/wiki/The_Prospect_Studios "The Prospect Studios") in [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), California. *General Hospital* was the second soap to air on ABC after the short-lived *[Road to Reality](/wiki/Road_to_Reality "Road to Reality")* (1960–1961). In 1964, a sister soap was created for *General Hospital*, *[The Young Marrieds](/wiki/The_Young_Marrieds "The Young Marrieds")*; it ran for two years and was canceled because of low ratings. *General Hospital* also spawned the daytime series *[Port Charles](/wiki/Port_Charles "Port Charles")* (1997–2003) and the primetime spin-off *[General Hospital: Night Shift](/wiki/General_Hospital:_Night_Shift "General Hospital: Night Shift")* (2007–2008).

In the late 1970s, storylines began to shift focus around the [Spencer](/wiki/Spencer_family_(General_Hospital) "Spencer family (General Hospital)") and [Quartermaine families](/wiki/Quartermaine_family "Quartermaine family"). From 1979 to 1988, *General Hospital* had more viewers than any other daytime soap opera. It rose to the top of the ratings in the early 1980s in part thanks to the monumentally popular "[supercouple](/wiki/Supercouple "Supercouple")" [Luke and Laura](/wiki/Luke_and_Laura "Luke and Laura"), whose 1981 wedding brought in 30 million viewers and remains the highest-rated hour in American soap opera history.[[3]](#cite_note-4)[[4]](#cite_note-www.ew.com-5) The soap opera is also known for its high-profile celebrity guest stars who have included, among others, [Roseanne Barr](/wiki/Roseanne_Barr "Roseanne Barr"), [James Franco](/wiki/James_Franco "James Franco") and [Elizabeth Taylor](/wiki/Elizabeth_Taylor "Elizabeth Taylor"). On April 23, 2009, *General Hospital* began broadcasting in [high-definition television](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television"), making it the first ABC soap opera to make such a transition.[[5]](#cite_note-HD_Start-6)

*General Hospital* is listed in *[Guinness World Records](/wiki/Guinness_World_Records "Guinness World Records")* as the [longest-running American soap opera in production](/wiki/List_of_longest-running_television_shows_by_category "List of longest-running television shows by category"), and the second in American history after *[Guiding Light](/wiki/Guiding_Light "Guiding Light")*.[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-worldrecord-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) Concurrently, it is the world's third-longest-running scripted drama series in production after British serials *[The Archers](/wiki/The_Archers "The Archers")* and *[Coronation Street](/wiki/Coronation_Street "Coronation Street")*, as well as the world's second-longest-running televised soap opera still in production. It is also the longest-running serial produced in Hollywood, and the longest-running entertainment program in ABC television history. *General Hospital* became the oldest ongoing American soap opera on September 17, 2010, following the final broadcast of [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")' *[As the World Turns](/wiki/As_the_World_Turns "As the World Turns")*. On April 14, 2011, ABC announced the cancellation of both *[All My Children](/wiki/All_My_Children "All My Children")* and *[One Life to Live](/wiki/One_Life_to_Live "One Life to Live")*, leaving *General Hospital* as the last remaining soap opera airing on the network after January 13, 2012.[[9]](#cite_note-10) The show celebrated its [50th anniversary](/wiki/General_Hospital%27s_50th_anniversary "General Hospital's 50th anniversary") on April 1, 2013, and its 15,000th episode on June 22, 2022. It holds the record for most [Daytime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award "Daytime Emmy Award") for [Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series"), with 14 wins. In 2007, the show was listed as one of *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine's "100 Best TV Shows of All-Time".[[10]](#cite_note-11)

## Show history

### Origins

*General Hospital* was created by [Frank and Doris Hursley](/wiki/Frank_and_Doris_Hursley "Frank and Doris Hursley") and premiered on April 1, 1963, replacing the canceled [game show](/wiki/Game_show "Game show") *[Yours for a Song](/wiki/Yours_for_a_Song "Yours for a Song")*. The first stories were mainly set on the seventh floor of General Hospital, in an unnamed midsize Eastern city (the name of the city, [Port Charles](/wiki/Port_Charles_(fictional_city) "Port Charles (fictional city)"), would not be mentioned until the late 1970s by headwriters [Eileen and Robert Mason Pollock](/wiki/Eileen_and_Robert_Mason_Pollock "Eileen and Robert Mason Pollock").[[11]](#cite_note-Port_Charles_name-12)). "They had this concept of the show that it was like a big wagon wheel—the spokes would be the characters and the hub would be the hospital", [John Beradino](/wiki/John_Beradino "John Beradino") ([Steve Hardy](/wiki/Steve_Hardy_(General_Hospital) "Steve Hardy (General Hospital)")) later reflected to *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")* in 1994.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### History

Main article: [History of *General Hospital*](/wiki/History_of_General_Hospital "History of General Hospital")

Launched in 1963, the first stories were mainly set at General Hospital in an unnamed midsized Eastern city. Storylines revolved around [Steve Hardy](/wiki/Steve_Hardy_(General_Hospital) "Steve Hardy (General Hospital)") ([John Beradino](/wiki/John_Beradino "John Beradino")) and his friend, Nurse [Jessie Brewer](/wiki/Jessie_Brewer "Jessie Brewer") ([Emily McLaughlin](/wiki/Emily_McLaughlin "Emily McLaughlin")). Jessie's turbulent marriage to the much-younger [Phil Brewer](/wiki/Phil_Brewer "Phil Brewer") (originally portrayed by [Roy Thinnes](/wiki/Roy_Thinnes "Roy Thinnes"); lastly by [Martin West](/wiki/Martin_West_(actor) "Martin West (actor)")) was the center of many early storylines. In 1964 [Audrey Hardy](/wiki/Audrey_Hardy "Audrey Hardy") ([Rachel Ames](/wiki/Rachel_Ames "Rachel Ames")), a flight attendant and sister of Nurse [Lucille](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1960s)#Lucille_March "List of General Hospital characters (1960s)") ([Lucille Wall](/wiki/Lucille_Wall "Lucille Wall")), came to town, and was the woman who won Steve's heart.

By the end of the 1970s, *General Hospital* was facing dire ratings when [executive producer](/wiki/Executive_producer "Executive producer") [Gloria Monty](/wiki/Gloria_Monty "Gloria Monty") was brought in to turn the show around. Monty is credited with creation of the first [supercouple](/wiki/Supercouple "Supercouple"), [Luke and Laura](/wiki/Luke_and_Laura "Luke and Laura"), played by [Anthony Geary](/wiki/Anthony_Geary "Anthony Geary") and [Genie Francis](/wiki/Genie_Francis "Genie Francis"). The end of their hour wedding on November 17, 1981, was the most-watched event in daytime serial history.[[4]](#cite_note-www.ew.com-5) During the 1980s, the series featured several high-profile action, adventure, and some science fiction-based storylines. Location shooting at sites including [Mount Rushmore](/wiki/Mount_Rushmore "Mount Rushmore") in [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"); [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls"); [Grand Ole Opry](/wiki/Grand_Ole_Opry "Grand Ole Opry") in [Nashville, Tennessee](/wiki/Nashville,_Tennessee "Nashville, Tennessee"); [Atlantic City, New Jersey](/wiki/Atlantic_City,_New_Jersey "Atlantic City, New Jersey"); Big Bear and Avalon ([Catalina Island](/wiki/Santa_Catalina_Island,_California "Santa Catalina Island, California")), California; and [San Antonio, Texas](/wiki/San_Antonio,_Texas "San Antonio, Texas") are some that propelled the story.

After Gloria Monty first left the series in 1987, *General Hospital* entered into a transitional phase that lasted until Wendy Riche took the position of executive producer in 1992. Under Riche, the show gained critical acclaim for its sensitive handling of [social issues](/wiki/Social_issue "Social issue"). In 1994, Riche started an annual Nurses' Ball, a fundraiser and [HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") awareness event both on the show and in real life. Later that year, a [heart transplantation](/wiki/Heart_transplantation "Heart transplantation") storyline involves the death of eight-year-old [B. J. Jones](/wiki/B._J._Jones "B. J. Jones") (daughter of [Dr. Tony Jones](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1980s) "List of General Hospital characters (1980s)") and [Bobbie Spencer](/wiki/Bobbie_Spencer "Bobbie Spencer")) in a bus crash and the subsequent donation of her heart to her dying cousin [Maxie Jones](/wiki/Maxie_Jones "Maxie Jones"). Shortly afterwards, [Monica Quartermaine](/wiki/Monica_Quartermaine "Monica Quartermaine") ([Leslie Charleson](/wiki/Leslie_Charleson "Leslie Charleson")) begins a long battle with breast cancer, which leads to her adopting [Emily Quartermaine](/wiki/Emily_Quartermaine "Emily Quartermaine"), the orphaned young daughter of Monica's friend from treatment. *General Hospital* was also praised for the love story of teenagers [Stone Cates](/wiki/Stone_Cates "Stone Cates") ([Michael Sutton](/wiki/Michael_Sutton_(actor) "Michael Sutton (actor)")) and [Robin Scorpio](/wiki/Robin_Scorpio "Robin Scorpio") ([Kimberly McCullough](/wiki/Kimberly_McCullough "Kimberly McCullough")). After a struggle that lasted throughout most of 1995, Stone dies from AIDS at the age of 19 and his death is followed by 17-year-old Robin having to deal with being HIV-positive as a result of their relationship. Sutton received a nomination for the [Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Supporting_Actor_in_a_Drama_Series "Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Supporting Actor in a Drama Series") and McCullough won a [Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Younger Actress in a Drama Series](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Younger_Actress_in_a_Drama_Series "Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Younger Actress in a Drama Series") award. ABC featured an Afterschool Special "Positive: A Journey Into AIDS" revolving around the AIDS story as well as *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* best selling novel *Robin's Diary*.

On Saturday, December 14, 1996, *General Hospital* aired its one of three primetime episodes, *[General Hospital: Twist of Fate](/wiki/General_Hospital:_Twist_of_Fate "General Hospital: Twist of Fate")*, which picked up where that Friday's episode had left off. The special centered on Laura's supposed death at the hands of [Stefan Cassadine](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1990s) "List of General Hospital characters (1990s)"). In 1997, the show's long-rumored spin-off materialized into the half-hour serial, *[Port Charles](/wiki/Port_Charles "Port Charles")*. The series' 11,000th episode aired on February 20, 2006.[[13]](#cite_note-EW_2006-02-14) On April 23, 2009, *General Hospital* became ABC's first regular daytime drama to be taped and broadcast in [High-definition television](/wiki/High-definition_television "High-definition television"), though the 2008 season of its primetime spinoff *[General Hospital: Night Shift](/wiki/General_Hospital:_Night_Shift "General Hospital: Night Shift")* was in high definition. This is the second daytime drama to move to high definition after [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")'s *[The Young and the Restless](/wiki/The_Young_and_the_Restless "The Young and the Restless")*. On February 23, 2010, the series aired its 12,000th episode.[[14]](#cite_note-sn.soapnet.go.com-15) On December 1, 2011, ABC confirmed that former *[One Life to Live](/wiki/One_Life_to_Live "One Life to Live")* executive producer [Frank Valentini](/wiki/Frank_Valentini "Frank Valentini") and head writer [Ron Carlivati](/wiki/Ron_Carlivati "Ron Carlivati") would replace longtime executive producer [Jill Farren Phelps](/wiki/Jill_Farren_Phelps "Jill Farren Phelps") and [Garin Wolf](/wiki/Garin_Wolf "Garin Wolf"), respectively, though Wolf would remain on as a regular writer. The change took effect on January 9, 2012.[[15]](#cite_note-16) The first episode under the direction of Valentini aired on February 1, 2012, with Carlivati's material beginning on February 21. Several storylines reminiscent of iconic story arcs of the past were created and popular characters returned to the show in order to commemorate the 50th anniversary of the series in 2013.[[16]](#cite_note-17) The serial celebrated 13,000 episodes on February 24, 2014,[[17]](#cite_note-Episode_13,000-18) and marked its 51st anniversary several weeks later on April 1.[[18]](#cite_note-51st_anniversary-19) Also in January 2014, ABC renewed Carlivati's contract with the soap.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) The series marked its 52nd anniversary on April 1, 2015, with a special episode revolving around the [Spencer family](/wiki/Spencer_family_(General_Hospital) "Spencer family (General Hospital)").[[21]](#cite_note-ABC7-22)[[22]](#cite_note-Broadway-23)[[23]](#cite_note-Soaps-24)[[24]](#cite_note-People-25)

In July 2015, it was revealed Carlivati was fired as head writer; [Shelly Altman](/wiki/Shelly_Altman "Shelly Altman") and [Jean Passanante](/wiki/Jean_Passanante "Jean Passanante") were hired as his successors.[[25]](#cite_note-Writing_Changes-26) On September 16, 2016, *Daytime Confidential* reported that Valentini, Passanante and Altman re-signed with the show.[[26]](#cite_note-27) On June 6, 2017, Passanante announced her decision to retire from the serial.[[27]](#cite_note-Passanante-28) On July 29, 2017, it was revealed through Passanante that breakdown writer [Chris Van Etten](/wiki/Chris_Van_Etten "Chris Van Etten") would be promoted to co-head writer as her successor.[[28]](#cite_note-29) On February 23, 2018, the serial aired its 14,000th episode.[[29]](#cite_note-GH_Ep14000-30) On July 30, 2019, Altman announced her retirement; breakdown writer Dan O'Connor was named as her successor, joining Van Etten as co-head writer.[[30]](#cite_note-Altman_retirement-31) On June 22, 2022, the soap aired its 15,000 episode; in celebration, the episode focused on Francis' [Laura Spencer](/wiki/Laura_Spencer_(General_Hospital) "Laura Spencer (General Hospital)").[[1]](#cite_note-GH_Ep15000-1)

On January 22, 2024, it was announced Van Etten and O'Connor had been dismissed from their positions as co-head writers;[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) former associate head writer Patrick Mulcahey and present script editor [Elizabeth Korte](/wiki/Elizabeth_Korte "Elizabeth Korte") were named as their replacements.[[33]](#cite_note-34) Per reports, material from the former regime aired into March 2024.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Five months later, it was announced Mulcahey had been dismissed from his position as head writer.[[35]](#cite_note-36)[[36]](#cite_note-37) Mulcahey's final credited episode aired on August 6; the following day, it was announced Van Etten would resume the role of co-head writer credit, with Cathy LePard named as associate head writer.[[37]](#cite_note-38)

### Production

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/1973_10th_Anniversary_General_Hospital.jpg/250px-1973_10th_Anniversary_General_Hospital.jpg)](/wiki/File:1973_10th_Anniversary_General_Hospital.jpg)

John Beradino and Emily McLaughlin celebrating the 10th anniversary of the show in 1973

*General Hospital* has aired on the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") television network and has been filmed in Hollywood since its inception. The show was filmed in the [Sunset Gower Studios](/wiki/Sunset_Gower_Studios "Sunset Gower Studios") from 1963 to the mid-1980s. It relocated in the 1980s to [The Prospect Studios](/wiki/The_Prospect_Studios "The Prospect Studios").

*General Hospital* has had a number of different distributors throughout the show's history. From its beginning until 1968, it was a co-production of [Plitt Theatres](/wiki/Plitt_Theatres "Plitt Theatres") and Selmur Productions. ABC bought the series outright in 1968 and its ownership passed from Selmur to American Broadcasting Companies, Inc., ABC's old separate conglomerate. Ownership of the soap was then passed in 1986 to [Capital Cities/ABC](/wiki/Capital_Cities/ABC "Capital Cities/ABC"), which was formed after the acquisition of ABC by a smaller media concern, Capital Cities Communications. [The Walt Disney Company](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") bought Capital Cities/ABC in 1996, and Disney has held ownership of the soap since then.

Production of *General Hospital* was suspended in March 2020, as a direct result of the [COVID-19 pandemic in the United States](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_United_States "COVID-19 pandemic in the United States").[[38]](#cite_note-TVL_2020_suspend-39) Production resumed on July 22 of the same year; new episodes began airing on August 3, 2020.[[39]](#cite_note-40) *General Hospital* was Disney's first series to go back into production during the pandemic.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Though the series originally focused on solely the medical staff at Port Charles' General Hospital, and starred [John Beradino](/wiki/John_Beradino "John Beradino") as [Dr. Steve Hardy](/wiki/Steve_Hardy_(General_Hospital) "Steve Hardy (General Hospital)") and [Emily McLaughlin](/wiki/Emily_McLaughlin "Emily McLaughlin") as [Nurse Jessie Brewer](/wiki/Jessie_Brewer "Jessie Brewer"), the series branched out and began to focus more on the people and families of the town of Port Charles rather than those solely in the hospital.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Port Charles is full of "dysfunctional family dynamics ... and family drama remains the focal point of this town."[[43]](#cite_note-44) The current families on the show include the quarreling and wealthy Quartermaine family, the mobster crime Corinthos family, the middle class Scorpio/Jones family, the aristocratic Cassadine family, and the adventurous Spencer family.

## Setting

Since the series began in 1963, Port Charles, [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), has been the setting for the show. The town exists in the same fictional universe as other soap opera settings such as [Llanview](/wiki/Llanview "Llanview") (*[One Life to Live](/wiki/One_Life_to_Live "One Life to Live")*), [Pine Valley](/wiki/Pine_Valley_(All_My_Children) "Pine Valley (All My Children)") (*[All My Children](/wiki/All_My_Children "All My Children")*), [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (*[Ryan's Hope](/wiki/Ryan%27s_Hope "Ryan's Hope")*), and [Corinth](/w/index.php?title=Corinth,_Pennsylvania,&action=edit&redlink=1 "Corinth, Pennsylvania, (page does not exist)") (*[Loving](/wiki/Loving_(TV_series) "Loving (TV series)")*). The same setting was also used for *General Hospital*'s spinoff, *[Port Charles](/wiki/Port_Charles "Port Charles")*.

* **General Hospital** is founded by Dr. Steve Hardy. It is a major employer in the city, and one of the largest medical facilities on the East Coast. In the 1990s, [Sonny Corinthos](/wiki/Sonny_Corinthos "Sonny Corinthos") donates an extra wing dedicated to AIDS research, and in the 2000s, [Carly Corinthos](/wiki/Carly_Corinthos "Carly Corinthos") donates a pediatric center for head neurology. In 2009, a fire destroys the hospital, which is rebuilt with money from the [Quartermaine family](/wiki/Quartermaine_family "Quartermaine family").
* **The Metro Court** is a prominent hotel in Port Charles, built by [Jasper Jacks](/wiki/Jasper_Jacks "Jasper Jacks") on the site of the Port Charles Hotel after it is destroyed in a fire in 2004, and named in honor of [Courtney Matthews](/wiki/Courtney_Matthews "Courtney Matthews"), who Jax was pursuing romantically. Carly Corinthos soon becomes his business partner, and later co-owns with [Kate Howard](/wiki/Kate_Howard "Kate Howard") when Jax sells his portion to her. The hotel boasts a skyline restaurant, spa, penthouse suites, and business offices. The current owners are Carly and [Olivia Falconeri](/wiki/Olivia_Falconeri "Olivia Falconeri").
* **Kelly's Diner** is founded by Joe "Paddy" Kelly, and becomes a vintage restaurant in the heart of Port Charles. It features boarding rooms upstairs which become homes to many Port Charles residents and guests over the years. The restaurant is operated by Paddy's wife [Rose Kelly](/wiki/Rose_Kelly_(General_Hospital) "Rose Kelly (General Hospital)") after his death, who later sells it to [Ruby Anderson](/wiki/Ruby_Anderson "Ruby Anderson") when Rose leaves town. When Ruby dies, she leaves the diner to her niece [Bobbie Spencer](/wiki/Bobbie_Spencer "Bobbie Spencer") and nephew [Luke Spencer](/wiki/Luke_Spencer "Luke Spencer").
* **The Haunted Star** is a yacht first owned by Luke Spencer, who receives the vessel as a wedding present in 1981. In 2003, the ship is turned into a casino by Luke and investors [Skye Chandler](/wiki/Skye_Chandler "Skye Chandler") and [Tracy Quartermaine](/wiki/Tracy_Quartermaine "Tracy Quartermaine"). In 2011, Luke's daughter [Lulu Spencer](/wiki/Lulu_Spencer "Lulu Spencer") purchases the boat, and in 2012 [Johnny Zacchara](/wiki/Johnny_Zacchara "Johnny Zacchara") invests to become co-owner. Together they turn the ship into a nightclub. The ship was destroyed in 2023.
* **The Floating Rib** is a bar located in downtown Port Charles, just a block away from General Hospital. Originally named Jake's, the bar has been a hotspot for the local nightlife since the early 1990s. [Coleman Ratcliffe](/wiki/Coleman_Ratcliffe "Coleman Ratcliffe") becomes the owner in 2002, and [Mac Scorpio](/wiki/Mac_Scorpio "Mac Scorpio") takes over in 2012. There was also a popular fine dining restaurant in Port Charles with the same name in the late 1970s/early 1980s. In 2020, a bomb destroys the restaurant and kills multiple patrons.

## Accolades and recognition

Main article: [List of General Hospital awards](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_awards "List of General Hospital awards")

*General Hospital*'s cast and crew have won many awards since 1974 when the [Daytime Emmy Awards](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award "Daytime Emmy Award") were created. In 2012, the [Creative Arts Emmy Awards](/wiki/Creative_Arts_Emmy_Award "Creative Arts Emmy Award") were created. *General Hospital* has won 16 Daytime Emmys for [Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series](/wiki/Daytime_Emmy_Award_for_Outstanding_Drama_Series "Daytime Emmy Award for Outstanding Drama Series").[[45]](#cite_note-47)

In June 2009, *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")* ranked "Luke and Laura's Wedding" at number forty-five on its list of the 100 Greatest Episodes.[[46]](#cite_note-48) In December 2023, *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* ranked *General Hospital* number eighty-eight on its list of "100 Greatest TV Shows of All Time". Citing the Luke and Laura pairing as reason for the soap's "stratospheric heights", the magazine also called the soap's celebration of it sixtieth anniversary a "feat".[[47]](#cite_note-49)

## Broadcast

During the 1960s, *General Hospital* earned decent ratings against the likes of *[To Tell the Truth](/wiki/To_Tell_the_Truth "To Tell the Truth")* and *[The Secret Storm](/wiki/The_Secret_Storm "The Secret Storm")* on [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"), but there was a decline as the 1970s came, especially when [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")'s *[Another World](/wiki/Another_World_(TV_series) "Another World (TV series)")* became highly popular. For two years, it also faced CBS's *[The Price Is Right](/wiki/The_Price_Is_Right "The Price Is Right")*, already a major hit. After continued mediocrity in the [Nielsen ratings](/wiki/Nielsen_ratings "Nielsen ratings"), [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") was prepared to cancel *General Hospital*, but decided to give it a second chance in 1978 when it expanded the show from an experimental 45 minutes to a full hour. However, the expansion came with an ultimatum to the producers that they had six months to improve the show's ratings. [Gloria Monty](/wiki/Gloria_Monty "Gloria Monty") was hired as the new executive producer, and on her first day, she spent an extra $100,000 re-taping four episodes. A miracle occurred thanks to Monty and head writer [Douglas Marland](/wiki/Douglas_Marland "Douglas Marland"); the show became the most-watched daytime drama by 1979, marking a rare instance of a daytime serial's comeback from near-extinction. During the wedding of [Luke and Laura](/wiki/Luke_and_Laura "Luke and Laura") on November 17, 1981, about 30 million people tuned in to watch them exchange vows and be cursed by [Elizabeth Taylor](/wiki/Elizabeth_Taylor "Elizabeth Taylor")'s [Helena Cassadine](/wiki/Helena_Cassadine "Helena Cassadine").

From 1979 to 1988, *General Hospital* remained number one in the ratings, competing against two game shows and two low-rated soaps on NBC—*[Texas](/wiki/Texas_(TV_series) "Texas (TV series)")* and *[Santa Barbara](/wiki/Santa_Barbara_(TV_series) "Santa Barbara (TV series)")*—and *[Guiding Light](/wiki/Guiding_Light "Guiding Light")* on CBS (although *Guiding Light* experienced a renaissance for a brief period in the middle of 1984, and became the #1-rated soap, briefly dethroning *General Hospital* from the top ratings spot). For the most part, however, *General Hospital* continued to triumph, even after the departure of popular actors [Anthony Geary](/wiki/Anthony_Geary "Anthony Geary") and [Genie Francis](/wiki/Genie_Francis "Genie Francis") in the mid-1980s. Although *[The Young and the Restless](/wiki/The_Young_and_the_Restless "The Young and the Restless")* took *General Hospital's* place as the highest-rated serial in 1989, *General Hospital* continued to maintain excellent ratings.

Even at its peak in the 1980s, *General Hospital* had been pre-empted in at least two markets in the United States. With the show still number one in the Nielsens, [WDTN](/wiki/WDTN "WDTN") in [Dayton, Ohio](/wiki/Dayton,_Ohio "Dayton, Ohio") preempted the series upon joining ABC in January 1980 in favor of *[Woody Woodpecker](/wiki/Woody_Woodpecker "Woody Woodpecker")* and *[Super Friends](/wiki/Super_Friends "Super Friends")* cartoons. Later, the station would air such shows as *Hour Magazine*, *[Geraldo](/wiki/Geraldo_(talk_show) "Geraldo (talk show)")* and *[Maury](/wiki/Maury_(talk_show) "Maury (talk show)")* in the show's timeslot until September 2000, when the station's new owners, Sunrise Broadcasting (which purchased the station from [Hearst Television](/wiki/Hearst_Television "Hearst Television") two years prior), pulled *Maury* from the station's schedule, due to what it called "community standards", and brought *General Hospital* back to Dayton. In [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") and [Plattsburgh, New York](/wiki/Plattsburgh_(city),_New_York "Plattsburgh (city), New York"), [WVNY](/wiki/WVNY "WVNY") dropped *General Hospital* from the schedule in the 1980s and would only bring it back in 1995. During that hiatus, *General Hospital* still aired on [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal")'s [CFCF-DT](/wiki/CFCF-DT "CFCF-DT"), whose signal was decently available in Vermont and Plattsburgh.

Ever since the 1991–1992 season of *General Hospital*, the show has had a steady decline in ratings. On and off, it would rank between third and fifth place in the Nielsen ratings, with CBS's *The Young And The Restless* and *The Bold and the Beautiful* coming in first and second place, respectively. *General Hospital* remained in between third and fifth place in the ratings during that time, and from late 1991 to 1996 *All My Children* held the title of ABC's highest-rated soap.

After months of speculation and cancellation rumors, *[Deadline Hollywood](/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood "Deadline Hollywood")* reported on April 11, 2012, that ABC quietly made the decision to keep *General Hospital* on the air and to cancel instead the lower-rated talk show *[The Revolution](/wiki/The_Revolution_(TV_program) "The Revolution (TV program)")*.[[48]](#cite_note-Revolution_canceled-50) On June 26, 2012, ABC officially announced that *General Hospital* would move to the 2 p.m. ET/PT timeslot starting on September 10, 2012, and that the network would give the 3:00 p.m. hour back to its affiliates, as it was the recommended time slot for [Katie Couric](/wiki/Katie_Couric "Katie Couric")'s new, ABC-[syndicated](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication") talk show, *[Katie](/wiki/Katie_(talk_show) "Katie (talk show)")*.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

Encore episodes were shown every weeknight on the former cable channel [Soapnet](/wiki/Soapnet "Soapnet"), with a marathon on Saturday and classic episodes at 4 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. (3 a.m. and 4 a.m. CST).

Production of *General Hospital* was suspended in March 2020 as a direct result of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United States. The show had banked roughly two months' worth of episodes at that time.[[38]](#cite_note-TVL_2020_suspend-39) By airing repeats on "Flashback Fridays", *General Hospital* was able to air original episodes through the week ending of May 21.[[50]](#cite_note-TVL_encore-52) This was followed by several weeks of vintage episode repeats.[[50]](#cite_note-TVL_encore-52) Production resumed on July 22, 2020, and new episodes began airing on August 3.[[51]](#cite_note-53)

On October 22, 2024, it was announced [10Play](/wiki/10Play "10Play"), a free [video-on-demand](/wiki/Video-on-demand "Video-on-demand") and [catch-up TV](/wiki/Catch-up_TV "Catch-up TV") service by [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10"), would carry the soap in Australia beginning January 1, 2025.[[52]](#cite_note-54) [Foxtel](/wiki/Foxtel "Foxtel")'s [W](/wiki/SoHo_(Australian_TV_channel) "SoHo (Australian TV channel)") previously broadcast the soap in 2011.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

## Parodies and references in other media

The popularity of *General Hospital* has gained it many parodies and references in other mainstream programs. For example:

* In the early 1990s, some episodes of *General Hospital* were featured as "shorts" during the fourth season of the parody show *[Mystery Science Theater 3000](/wiki/Mystery_Science_Theater_3000 "Mystery Science Theater 3000")*.
* The series was also parodied/homaged in the song "General Hospi-Tale" by [The Afternoon Delights](/wiki/The_Afternoon_Delights "The Afternoon Delights"),[[59]](#cite_note-NYT_25-62) and in the film *[Tootsie](/wiki/Tootsie "Tootsie")*, which took place among the cast and crew of a fictional soap opera program.
* In the [Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company") medical drama *[House](/wiki/House_(TV_series) "House (TV series)")*, [Gregory House](/wiki/Gregory_House "Gregory House") enjoys *Prescription Passion*, which is an over-the-top parody of *General Hospital* that he watches constantly. In the season three episode, "[Half-Wit](/wiki/Half-Wit "Half-Wit")", House hides his blood test results under the name, "Luke N. Laura", referring to *General Hospital*'s popular couple.
* In the 1970s, *[The Muppet Show](/wiki/The_Muppet_Show "The Muppet Show")* had a recurring sketch called "Veterinarian's Hospital" parodying the hospital soap opera/drama genre clearly inspired by *General Hospital* and shows like it.
* *[Mad TV](/wiki/Mad_TV "Mad TV")* did a sketch on the series with actors [Jacklyn Zeman](/wiki/Jacklyn_Zeman "Jacklyn Zeman"), [Rebecca Herbst](/wiki/Rebecca_Herbst "Rebecca Herbst"), and [Jacob Young](/wiki/Jacob_Young "Jacob Young").
* The 1982 comedy film *[Young Doctors in Love](/wiki/Young_Doctors_in_Love "Young Doctors in Love")* featured a large part of General Hospital's cast from 1982.
* The February 23, 2000 episode of *[Late Show with David Letterman](/wiki/Late_Show_with_David_Letterman "Late Show with David Letterman")* covers a *faux* segment of the program with regards to [David Letterman](/wiki/David_Letterman#Heart_surgery_hiatus "David Letterman")'s heart surgery.[[60]](#cite_note-63)
* In a 2010 episode of *[The Colbert Report](/wiki/The_Colbert_Report "The Colbert Report")*, comedian [Stephen Colbert](/wiki/Stephen_Colbert "Stephen Colbert") poked fun at the show, responding to a clip of [Maurice Benard](/wiki/Maurice_Benard "Maurice Benard")'s [Sonny Corinthos](/wiki/Sonny_Corinthos "Sonny Corinthos") shooting [Dominic Zamprogna](/wiki/Dominic_Zamprogna "Dominic Zamprogna")'s [Dante Falconeri](/wiki/Dante_Falconeri "Dante Falconeri"), satirically screaming, "Sonny shot Dante! No!"[[61]](#cite_note-Colbert_Report_7/6/10-64)
* In the ABC comedy *[The Goldbergs](/wiki/The_Goldbergs_(2013_TV_series) "The Goldbergs (2013 TV series)")*, Erica and Adam frequently watch *General Hospital* together.
* In the book series *Virgin River* by [Robyn Carr](/wiki/Robyn_Carr "Robyn Carr"), Mel mentions watching *General Hospital* in nursing school.[[62]](#cite_note-65)

## Spin-offs and specials

The success of the long-running soap opera has had one sister serial, one spinoff in the United States, and two primetime spinoffs in the United States and the United Kingdom.

*[The Young Marrieds](/wiki/The_Young_Marrieds "The Young Marrieds")* was ABC's first attempt at a sister serial for *General Hospital*. It ran for only two years, racking up a total of 380 episodes. Despite its moderate popularity, it was aired against [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")'s top-rated *[The Edge of Night](/wiki/The_Edge_of_Night "The Edge of Night")*, which it could not compete against. The series finale aired on March 25, 1966, with the show's main protagonist contemplating suicide. It ended in a cliffhanger, leaving the audience wondering if the man had killed himself or not. *The Young Marrieds* was set in the fictional suburb of Queen's Point, which was considered by the writers to be a suburb of *[Port Charles](/wiki/Port_Charles "Port Charles")*.

The British television series *[General Hospital](/wiki/General_Hospital_(British_TV_series) "General Hospital (British TV series)")* did not feature any characters from the American show, but was modeled after its format. It started as a half-hour program broadcast in the afternoons, which was unusual for British serials that normally aired in prime time. In 1975, it was expanded to an hour-long format and moved to Friday evenings.

*Port Charles* was a daytime drama that initially featured interns in a competitive medical school program,[[63]](#cite_note-PC_Spinoff-66) and was known for having more action actually in the hospital than *General Hospital* itself. It also included the characters of [Scott Baldwin](/wiki/Scott_Baldwin "Scott Baldwin"), [Serena Baldwin](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1990s) "List of General Hospital characters (1990s)"), [Lucy Coe](/wiki/Lucy_Coe "Lucy Coe"), [Kevin Collins](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1990s) "List of General Hospital characters (1990s)"), and [Karen Wexler](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1990s) "List of General Hospital characters (1990s)"), all of whom originally appeared as characters on *General Hospital*.[[63]](#cite_note-PC_Spinoff-66) As the show evolved, it tended more towards gothic intrigue, including supernatural elements such as [vampires](/wiki/Vampire "Vampire") and [afterlife](/wiki/Afterlife "Afterlife"). It also switched formats from an open-ended daytime serial to 13-week story arcs known as "books", similar to Spanish-language [telenovelas](/wiki/Telenovela "Telenovela").

*[General Hospital: Night Shift](/wiki/General_Hospital:_Night_Shift "General Hospital: Night Shift")* is the second American prime time spinoff of a daytime drama (the first being *[Our Private World](/wiki/Our_Private_World "Our Private World")*, a spinoff of *As the World Turns*). Its first season aired from July 12, 2007, to October 4, 2007, on [Soapnet](/wiki/Soapnet "Soapnet"), a cable channel owned by ABC.[[64]](#cite_note-SC_Feb_2007-67) The series follows the nighttime adventures of familiar and new characters around the hospital. As of March 2008, the first season of the series was "Soapnet's most-watched series ever", with ABC Daytime and Soapnet President [Brian Frons](/wiki/Brian_Frons "Brian Frons") noting that *Night Shift* drew more than 1 million new viewers to the channel during its first season.[[65]](#cite_note-THR_2008-05-27-68)

*[General Hospital: Twist of Fate](/wiki/General_Hospital:_Twist_of_Fate "General Hospital: Twist of Fate")* was a primetime special that aired on Saturday, December 14, 1996. The episode picked up where that Friday's show had left off. The special centered on Laura's supposed death at the hands of [Stefan Cassadine](/wiki/List_of_General_Hospital_characters_(1990s) "List of General Hospital characters (1990s)").[[66]](#cite_note-69)

On April 2, 1998, *General Hospital* aired a primetime special in celebration of the program's 35th anniversary. Hosted by [Anthony Geary](/wiki/Anthony_Geary "Anthony Geary"), the show focused and recapped on many popular storylines including [Monica Quartermaine](/wiki/Monica_Quartermaine "Monica Quartermaine")'s breast cancer, [BJ](/wiki/Children_of_General_Hospital "Children of General Hospital")'s death, and [Stone Cates](/wiki/Stone_Cates "Stone Cates")' battle with HIV. This was the first anniversary special that was broadcast in primetime and that did not include any of the current storyline.[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[68]](#cite_note-71)

On April 6, 2013, as part of the show's 50th anniversary commemoration, ABC's newsmagazine *[20/20](/wiki/20/20_(American_TV_program) "20/20 (American TV program)")* aired *General Hospital: The Real Soap Dish*—a retrospective and behind-the-scenes special hosted by [Katie Couric](/wiki/Katie_Couric "Katie Couric").[[69]](#cite_note-72)

On September 5, 2014, it was announced that cast member [Nancy Lee Grahn](/wiki/Nancy_Lee_Grahn "Nancy Lee Grahn") would begin to host a companion web series for ABC.com in January 2015, *General Hospital Now!*, which would feature behind-the-scenes interviews with fellow cast members, as well as panel discussions with comedians who are fans of the show.[[70]](#cite_note-73)[[71]](#cite_note-74)[[72]](#cite_note-75)

On May 15 and 18, 2015, *General Hospital* aired two live episodes as part of its 52nd anniversary celebration, using the [hashtag](/wiki/Hashtag "Hashtag") #GHLive to promote the broadcast on social media.[[73]](#cite_note-76)

On December 15, 2023, it was announced ABC would air a primetime special *General Hospital: 60 Years of Stars and Storytelling*, in celebration of the soap's sixtieth anniversary. The special featured interviews with present and former cast members, behind-the-scenes footage, blooper reels and a "special fan tribute".[[74]](#cite_note-77) The special aired on January 4, 2024.[[75]](#cite_note-78)
